title: Andaman & Nicobar: A Union Territory of Islands
topic: Geography and Administration of A&N Islands
text: The Andaman and Nicobar Islands form a union territory of India situated in the eastern part of the Bay of Bengal. The archipelago consists of more than five hundred islands, of which only a few are inhabited. Port Blair serves as the capital and the administrative headquarters of the territory. The islands are known for their pristine beaches, rich marine biodiversity, and dense tropical rainforests. The Andaman and Nicobar Administration is responsible for governance, development, and welfare of the population spread across these remote islands. The territory has a Lieutenant Governor appointed by the President of India who oversees the administrative functions. Public employment in this union territory is governed by the Andaman and Nicobar Administration service rules. Candidates aspiring for clerical positions are required to appear in competitive examinations conducted by the administration. The Combined Higher Secondary Level examination is one of the primary recruitment drives for filling posts such as Lower Division Clerk in various government offices across the islands. Efficient administration requires skilled clerical staff who can handle correspondence, maintain records, and support senior officers in their daily functions.

title: Marine Biodiversity of the Bay of Bengal
text: The waters surrounding the Andaman and Nicobar Islands are among the most biologically diverse marine environments in the world. The coral reefs that fringe the islands support a staggering variety of fish, invertebrates, and other marine organisms. These reefs serve as nurseries for commercially important fish species and protect the coastline from erosion caused by wave action. The dugong, also known as the sea cow, is one of the endangered marine mammals that inhabits the shallow coastal waters around the islands. Sea turtles of several species nest on the sandy beaches of the archipelago, including the leatherback, which is the largest of all living sea turtles. The territorial waters of the Andaman and Nicobar Islands have been declared a national park and biosphere reserve in recognition of their exceptional ecological significance. Human activities such as fishing, tourism, and coastal development must be carefully managed to avoid damaging these fragile ecosystems. Climate change poses an additional threat through ocean warming and acidification, which cause coral bleaching and reduce the resilience of reef communities. The administration has implemented several conservation programmes to protect the marine heritage of these islands for future generations.

title: India's Digital Governance Revolution
text: India has been at the forefront of digital governance transformation over the past decade, deploying technology to improve the efficiency, transparency, and accessibility of public services. The Digital India initiative launched by the government in 2015 has been instrumental in creating digital infrastructure, promoting digital literacy, and delivering government services electronically to citizens across the country. The introduction of the Aadhaar biometric identification system has enabled direct benefit transfers, eliminating leakages in the delivery of subsidies and welfare payments. The Unified Payments Interface has revolutionised digital financial transactions, making it possible for millions of Indians to transfer money instantly using their smartphones without requiring access to a bank branch. Government offices at the central and state levels have adopted electronic file management systems, significantly reducing the reliance on paper documentation. The Andaman and Nicobar Administration has also embraced digital governance, using online portals for recruitment, certificate issuance, and public grievance redressal. Clerks and support staff in government offices are increasingly expected to be proficient in the use of computer applications, internet-based services, and official software platforms. The ability to type accurately and efficiently is a fundamental requirement for clerical personnel in the modern digital government environment.

title: India's Economic Development Story
text: India's economic development over the past three decades represents one of the most remarkable transformations in the history of emerging market economies. Following the liberalisation of the economy in 1991, India shed much of its regulatory burden and opened its markets to foreign investment and trade. This triggered a period of sustained growth that lifted hundreds of millions of people out of poverty and created a substantial middle class. The information technology and business process outsourcing sectors emerged as major contributors to economic growth, establishing India as a global hub for software development and technology services. Agriculture continues to be the primary occupation of a large proportion of the rural population, though its share of national output has declined relative to industry and services. The government has pursued infrastructure development as a strategic priority, recognising that roads, power, ports, and digital connectivity are essential prerequisites for sustainable growth. Schemes such as Make in India and the Production Linked Incentive programme are designed to boost domestic manufacturing and increase India's share of global export markets. Financial inclusion initiatives have brought banking services and insurance to previously underserved communities in rural and semi-urban areas. Continued investment in education, health, and skill development is essential for sustaining growth and ensuring that its benefits are equitably distributed across all segments of society.

title: Science and Technology in Everyday Life
text: Science and technology permeate virtually every aspect of modern life, from the smartphones we carry to the medical treatments we receive and the food we eat. The rapid advancement of computing power over recent decades has enabled innovations that would have seemed impossible even a generation ago. Artificial intelligence systems can now diagnose medical conditions, translate languages, recognise speech, and drive vehicles with a degree of accuracy that rivals or even surpasses human performance. Biotechnology has transformed agriculture by enabling the development of crops that are resistant to pests, diseases, and adverse climatic conditions, helping to secure food supply in an era of population growth and environmental uncertainty. Renewable energy technologies such as solar panels and wind turbines have become competitive with fossil fuels in many markets, opening the possibility of transitioning to a clean energy economy that does not contribute to global warming. Space exploration has delivered practical benefits in the form of weather forecasting satellites, navigation systems, and communication infrastructure that is now taken for granted in daily life. The Andaman and Nicobar Islands have benefited from satellite-based connectivity, bringing internet and communication services to remote communities that were previously isolated. Encouraging interest in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics among young students is essential for building the knowledge economy of the future.
